notes: ML data-quality paper about NBP prediction. The bulk of the paper concerns model architectures, featurization methods, and aggregate performance metrics (MAE, MAPE, R^2, Delta MAPE) on training/validation/test sets of 1259-DIPPR and 4799-SPEED compounds. These aggregate stats are excluded per task instructions. The paper does, however, contain a small number of per-compound NBP values in the Introduction, given as illustrative examples of unreliable SPEED database entries vs literature reference values. Two compounds are named with explicit NBP values: (1) 4-phenyldiazenylbenzene-1,3-diamine, cited at 2535.15 K (from SPEED, flagged as implausible); (2) chlorine, cited at 993.15 K (from SPEED, flagged as wrong) and at 239 K (literature reference value). These are per-compound named values in the main text and should be included in the recall enumeration. No other compounds in the main text carry explicit property values: hexane and propanal are mentioned in the Joback-groups section only to describe their functional group decomposition, with no NBP value reported. No experimental/synthesis section exists (this is a modeling paper). The 376-compound test set, 1259-DIPPR training set, and 4799-SPEED training set values themselves are not enumerated in the main text — only summary statistics are reported. Conclusion: original 0-row emission was INCORRECT; should have been 3.

items.evidence_quote: For example, 4-phenyldiazenylbenzene-1,3-diamine is said to have an NBP of 2535.15 K. For this relatively small compound, this value is questionable, as the chemical would likely decompose or combust at a much lower temperature, and thus the number cannot be considered experimental or reliable.
items.evidence_location: Introduction, paragraph discussing SPEED database unreliability

items.evidence_quote: Another example of unreliable data in this set is the NBP of chlorine, which is stated to be 993.15 K—far above the 239 K reported by multiple sources.
